source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 16.2.1 Rules - priorities & actions (pp. 305-307)

questions.q: Which lists the syslog priorities from LOWEST to HIGHEST?
options: debug, info, notice, warning, err, crit, alert, emerg
emerg, alert, crit, err, warning, notice, info, debug
info, debug, warning, err, notice, crit, emerg, alert
err, warning, info, debug, notice, crit, alert, emerg
questions.why: Lowest to highest: debug < info < notice < warning < err < crit < alert < emerg.

questions.q: Lower vs higher priority - what is the trade-off in what gets logged?
options: Lower priority records MORE detail (less severe); higher priority records LESS but more serious
Higher priority records more detail
Priority has no effect on volume
Lower priority means more severe
questions.why: The lower the priority, the more (and less severe) the entries; the higher, the fewer and more serious.

questions.q: In a selector, what does 'cron.info' match versus 'cron.=info'?
options: cron.info matches info AND higher priorities; cron.=info matches ONLY info
They are identical
cron.info matches only info; cron.=info matches info and higher
cron.=info excludes info
questions.why: A plain '.' means that priority and above; '.=' means exactly that priority; '.!' excludes it.

questions.q: In the action field, what does '@192.168.100.20' mean?
options: Send the log to a remote machine at that address
Save to a local file
Send to all logged-in users
Write to a terminal
questions.why: A leading @ forwards to a remote host; a path saves locally, :omusrmsg: sends to users, /dev/ttyN to a terminal.

questions.q: In the action field, what does a leading '-' before a filename mean (e.g. -/var/log/maillog)?
options: Disable the buffering (sync) mechanism when writing to the file
Delete the file first
Make the file read-only
Comment out the rule
questions.why: The leading - disables synchronous writes (buffering) to that file, improving performance for busy logs.
